We further conduct inductive content analysis on code snippets from California, Utah, and North Dakota to identify qualitative patterns in code quality violations. Results show that readability violations are the most prevalent across all languages, followed by reliability, performance, and security. Common issues include improper whitespace, inconsistent formatting, program-flow errors, inefficient resource use, unsanitised inputs, and insecure dynamic evaluation. Regional analysis indicates that major technology hubs produce more parsable snippets but do not necessarily exhibit higher violation densities. States with broader access to computing devices, Internet subscriptions, higher income, and more equitable wealth distribution tend to show fewer code quality violations. Qualitative findings suggest that established technology regions often produce more complex violations, while less mature technology regions display more fundamental errors. These findings highlight the socio-technical nature of code quality in community question-answering platforms and suggest that developers should exercise caution when reusing online code snippets.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates code quality of Stack Overflow snippets across different languages and regions, finding readability as the most common issue and highlighting socio-technical influences on code quality.
AI.motivation: To understand the quality of code snippets reused by developers from Stack Overflow, particularly across different programming languages and geographic contexts.
AI.method: The study uses linting and static analysis tools to assess code snippets on dimensions of reliability, readability, performance, and security. It involves evaluating violations by region and conducting qualitative content analysis on selected states.
AI.result: Readability violations are the most common, with reliability, performance, and security violations also identified. Regional analysis shows that tech hubs produce more parsable snippets, but not necessarily fewer violations.
AI.conclusion: The findings underscore the socio-technical nature of code quality, indicating that developers should be cautious when reusing snippets, especially from diverse geographic backgrounds.

title: Synthetic Data Generation for Long-Tail Medical Image Classification: A Case Study in Skin Lesions
summary: Long-tailed class distributions are pervasive in multi-class medical datasets and pose significant challenges for deep learning models which typically underperform on tail classes with limited samples. This limitation is particularly problematic in medical applications, where rare classes often correspond to severe or high-risk diseases and therefore require high diagnostic accuracy. Existing solutions-including specialized architectures, rebalanced loss functions, and handcrafted data augmentation-offer only marginal improvements and struggle to scale due to their limited and largely deterministic variability. To address these challenges, we introduce a diffusion-model-driven synthetic data augmentation pipeline tailored for medical long-tailed classification. Our approach features a novel inpainting diffusion model combined with an Out-of-Distribution (OOD) post-selection mechanism to ensure diverse, realistic, and clinically meaningful synthetic samples. Evaluated on the ISIC2019 skin lesion classification dataset, one of the largest and most imbalanced medical imaging benchmarks, our method yields substantial improvements in overall performance, with particularly pronounced gains on tail classes with more than $28\%$ improvement on the class with the fewest samples. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of diffusion-based augmentation in mitigating long-tail imbalance and enhancing medical classification robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a diffusion-model-based synthetic data augmentation pipeline that enhances deep learning performance on long-tailed medical datasets, particularly improving accuracy for tail classes.
AI.motivation: Long-tailed distributions in medical datasets hinder deep learning model performance, especially for rare high-risk diseases that need accurate diagnosis.
AI.method: A synthetic data augmentation pipeline using a novel inpainting diffusion model along with an Out-of-Distribution post-selection mechanism for generating diverse and clinically relevant samples.
AI.result: The approach was tested on the ISIC2019 dataset, yielding substantial overall performance improvements, particularly over 28% on the tail class with the fewest samples.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion-based augmentation effectively addresses long-tail imbalances, enhancing the robustness of medical classification models.

title: PHANTOM: Polymorphic Honeytoken Adaptation with Narrative-Tailored Organisational Mimicry
summary: Honeytokens, decoy digital assets planted to detect and attribute unauthorised access, are a well-established primitive in cyber deception. Existing generation tools produce static, template-based tokens that lack organisational specificity and are identifiable by statistical, syntactic, and semantic analysis. We introduce PHANTOM (Polymorphic Honeytoken Adaptation with Narrative-Tailored Organisational Mimicry), a framework that generates contextually convincing honeytokens by encoding organisation-specific knowledge: domain names, service naming conventions, technology-stack idioms, and realistic secret-value distributions, into a multi-component generation pipeline. We formalise honeytoken quality through a four-component Believability Score that captures syntactic validity, semantic coherence, statistical plausibility, and human acceptance. We use this metric to evaluate PHANTOM across 8 token types and 4 organisational contexts against a template-based baseline. PHANTOM achieves B = 0.778 +/- 0.057 versus B = 0.576 +/- 0.058 for templates (Delta = +0.203, t = 14.07, p < 0.001, Cohen's d = 3.52). Human-evaluator acceptance rises from 6.2% to 100%, and detection resistance (DR = 1 - Pd) improves from 0.609 to 0.870 across three simulated scanner models (regex, entropy analysis, and ML classifier), each with p < 0.001. The semantic coherence gap (Delta Sc = +0.309, d = 4.52) is the dominant driver, confirming the hypothesis that organisational context is the critical missing ingredient in current approaches. All results are reproduced without external API calls, making the pipeline fully deployable in air-gapped environments.

AI.tldr: PHANTOM improves honeytoken generation by introducing organisation-specific context, leading to increased believability and resistance to detection compared to traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing honeytoken generation tools, which produce identifiable static tokens, by creating contextually relevant tokens that better mimic an organisation's digital environment.
AI.method: PHANTOM employs a multi-component pipeline that incorporates organisation-specific knowledge into honeytoken generation, and it evaluates quality through a Believability Score based on syntactic, semantic, statistical, and human acceptance metrics.
AI.result: PHANTOM demonstrates a significantly higher Believability Score (B = 0.778) compared to template-based tokens (B = 0.576), with marked improvements in human acceptance from 6.2% to 100% and detection resistance ratings increasing from 0.609 to 0.870.
AI.conclusion: The results validate the hypothesis that incorporating organisational context is crucial for effective honeytoken strategies, enhancing their deployment capabilities even in restricted environments.

title: S^2tory: Story Spine Distillation for Movie Script Summarization
summary: Movie scripts pose a fundamental challenge for automatic summarization due to their non-linear, cross-cut narrative structure, which makes surface-level saliency methods ineffective at preserving core story progression. To address this, we introduce S^2tory (Story Spine Distillation), a narratology-grounded framework that leverages character development trajectories to identify plot nuclei, the essential events that drive the narrative forward, while filtering out peripheral satellite events that merely enrich atmosphere or emotion. Our Narrative Expert Agent (NEAgent) performs theory-constrained reasoning, whose distilled knowledge conditions a small model to identify plot nuclei. Another model then uses these plot nuclei to generate the summary. Experiments on the MovieSum dataset demonstrate state-of-the-art semantic fidelity at approximately 3.5x compression, and zero-shot evaluation on BookSum confirms strong out-of-domain generalization. Human evaluation further validates that narratological theory provides an indispensable foundation for modeling complex, non-linear narratives.

AI.tldr: The paper presents S^2tory, a framework for automatic summarization of movie scripts, which identifies key plot events through character trajectories, resulting in high-quality summaries.
AI.motivation: Automatic summarization of movie scripts is challenging due to their non-linear narratives and the limitations of traditional saliency methods.
AI.method: The S^2tory framework utilizes character development trajectories to identify plot nuclei while filtering out less relevant events, employing a Narrative Expert Agent for reasoning, followed by summarization from identified nuclei.
AI.result: Experiments reveal state-of-the-art semantic fidelity in summaries with 3.5x compression on the MovieSum dataset and strong generalization in zero-shot settings on BookSum.
AI.conclusion: The use of narratological theory is crucial for accurately modeling and summarizing complex movie narratives.

title: A Near-Field Compatible Model for 2D Waveguide-Fed Metasurfaces
summary: This paper presents a novel physically consistent analytical model for two-dimensional (2D) waveguide-fed metasurface antennas that is based on the discrete dipole approximation. The proposed framework extends previous works deriving power-consistent constraints on the magnetic polarizability tensor, leading to closed-form expressions for the effective polarizabilities. The model is validated through full-wave simulations for multi-feed settings, and is extended to a near-field compatible~formulation enabling accurate predictions in the radiating near-field.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new analytical model for 2D waveguide-fed metasurface antennas based on discrete dipole approximation, providing effective polarizabilities and near-field predictions.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate model for waveguide-fed metasurface antennas that ensures physical consistency and improves upon existing methods.
AI.method: The model employs the discrete dipole approximation and incorporates power-consistent constraints for the magnetic polarizability tensor, resulting in closed-form effective polarizability expressions.
AI.result: The model is validated with full-wave simulations for multiple feed configurations and adapted for near-field applications to enhance predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a robust framework for analyzing 2D metasurface antennas, improving predictive capabilities in both far-field and near-field scenarios.

title: Bridging the Embodiment Gap: Disentangled Cross-Embodiment Video Editing
summary: Learning robotic manipulation from human videos is a promising solution to the data bottleneck in robotics, but the distribution shift between humans and robots remains a critical challenge. Existing approaches often produce entangled representations, where task-relevant information is coupled with human-specific kinematics, limiting their adaptability. We propose a generative framework for cross-embodiment video editing that directly addresses this by learning explicitly disentangled task and embodiment representations. Our method factorizes a demonstration video into two orthogonal latent spaces by enforcing a dual contrastive objective: it minimizes mutual information between the spaces to ensure independence while maximizing intra-space consistency to create stable representations. A parameter-efficient adapter injects these latent codes into a frozen video diffusion model, enabling the synthesis of a coherent robot execution video from a single human demonstration, without requiring paired cross-embodiment data. Experiments show our approach generates temporally consistent and morphologically accurate robot demonstrations, offering a scalable solution to leverage internet-scale human video for robot learning.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of learning robotic manipulation from human videos by proposing a generative framework that disentangles task and embodiment representations, allowing robots to execute tasks demonstrated by humans without requiring paired data.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to overcome the data bottleneck in robotics while tackling the distribution shift between human and robot kinematics.
AI.method: A generative framework is introduced that factorizes a demonstration video into two independent latent spaces using a dual contrastive objective, which enhances representation stability and independence.
AI.result: The method enables the synthesis of coherent robot execution videos from human demonstrations, demonstrating temporally consistent and morphologically accurate robot behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed solution effectively leverages human video content at scale for enhancing robot learning capability.

title: Deep Graph-Language Fusion for Structure-Aware Code Generation
summary:
Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) have the potential to transform software development tasks. However, despite significant advances, current PLMs struggle to capture the structured and relational attributes of code, such as control flow and data dependencies. This limitation is rooted in an architectural mismatch: whereas code structure is best represented by graphs, transformer-based LLMs process input as sequential token patterns and therefore lack explicit structural awareness. While recent research has explored integrating graph-based code representations using techniques like graph feature extraction, retrieval-augmented generation, and prompt engineering, existing approaches suffer from information loss during dense feature extraction or prompt encoding; notably, the potential of deep, token-level fusion of graph features within model internals has not been systematically explored.
  In this paper, we initiate such an exploration by introducing CGFuse, a novel framework that enables token-level integration of graph-derived representations by infusing learned graph features directly into the intermediate layers of pre-trained language models. CGFuse combines a graph neural network (GNN) with a language model to explicitly preserve and exploit fine-grained structural information from code graphs, including abstract syntax trees and data-flow graphs. We systematically evaluate CGFuse across multiple LLMs, demonstrating up to 10-16% BLEU and 6-11% CodeBLEU improvements in code generation performance. These results highlight the potential of deep graph-PLM integration to advance the field toward more robust, capable AI-driven software development.

AI.tldr: CGFuse is a novel framework that integrates graph-derived representations into pre-trained language models, enhancing their ability to capture code structure and improve code generation performance.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current pre-trained language models in capturing the structured attributes of code, specifically control flow and data dependencies, which traditional sequential processing does not adequately handle.
AI.method: CGFuse introduces a method for token-level integration of graph features into language models by infusing graph representations directly into their intermediate layers, using a combination of a graph neural network (GNN) and a language model.
AI.result: Evaluation of CGFuse across multiple large language models (LLMs) shows improvements in code generation performance, with up to 10-16% increases in BLEU scores and 6-11% increases in CodeBLEU scores.
AI.conclusion: The integration of deep graph features within pre-trained language models has the potential to significantly enhance their performance in software development tasks, paving the way for more robust AI systems.

title: CropVLM: A Domain-Adapted Vision-Language Model for Open-Set Crop Analysis
summary: High-throughput plant phenotyping, the quantitative measurement of observable plant traits, is critical for modern breeding but remains constrained by a "phenotyping bottleneck," where manual data collection is labor-intensive and prone to observer bias. Conventional closed-set computer vision systems fail to address this challenge, as they require extensive species-specific annotation and lack the flexibility to handle diverse breeding populations. To bridge this gap, we present CropVLM, a Vision-Language Model (VLM) adapted for the agricultural domain via Domain-Specific Semantic Alignment (DSSA). Trained on 52,987 manually selected image-caption pairs covering 37 species in natural field conditions, CropVLM effectively maps agronomic terminology to fine-grained visual features. We further introduce the Hybrid Open-Set Localization Network (HOS-Net), an architecture that integrates CropVLM to enable the detection of novel crops solely from natural language descriptions without retraining. By eliminating the reliance on species-specific training data, CropVLM provides a scalable solution for high-throughput phenotyping, accelerating genetic gain and facilitating large-scale biodiversity research essential for sustainable agriculture. The trained model weights and complete pipeline implementation are publicly available at: [https://github.com/boudiafA/CropVLM](https://github.com/boudiafA/CropVLM). In comprehensive evaluations, CropVLM achieves 72.51% zero-shot classification accuracy, outperforming seven CLIP-style baselines. Our detection pipeline demonstrates superior zero-shot generalization to novel species, achieving 49.17 AP50 on our CVTCropDet benchmark and 50.73 AP50 on tropical fruit species, compared to 34.89 and 48.58 for the next-best method, respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CropVLM, a Vision-Language Model for high-throughput plant phenotyping, addressing limitations of conventional methods through Domain-Specific Semantic Alignment, achieving high accuracy in species detection.
AI.motivation: To overcome the phenotyping bottleneck in plant breeding caused by manual data collection and limitations of closed-set computer vision systems.
AI.method: CropVLM is trained on 52,987 image-caption pairs across 37 plant species, utilizing Domain-Specific Semantic Alignment and integrating with the Hybrid Open-Set Localization Network for flexible detection of novel species based on natural language.
AI.result: CropVLM achieves 72.51% zero-shot classification accuracy and outperforms several baselines; its detection pipeline demonstrates significant zero-shot generalization for new species.
AI.conclusion: CropVLM offers a scalable and effective solution for high-throughput phenotyping, enhancing genetic research and sustainable agriculture.

title: Distributed Deep Variational Approach for Privacy-preserving Data Release
summary: Federated learning (FL) lets distributed nodes train a shared model without exchanging their raw data, but in privacy-sensitive deployments medical sensors, IoT devices, wearables the protection offered by keeping data local is incomplete: gradients, model updates, and the released representations themselves can leak sensitive attributes. We propose the \emph{Gaussian Privacy Protector} (GPP), a data-release framework for continuous, high-dimensional inputs that learns a stochastic encoder mapping raw data to a low-dimensional sanitized representation. The encoder is trained against a variational lower bound on the mutual information between the released representation and a designated sensitive attribute, while a separate cross-entropy term preserves a designated utility attribute, with a Lagrange multiplier $β$ controlling the trade-off. We then extend GPP to the federated setting, in which each client trains a local encoder, sensitive labels never leave the client, and the aggregator receives only sanitized representations giving instance-level privacy protection in addition to the standard ``raw data stays local'' guarantee of FL. We evaluate GPP on MNIST (digit-sum utility, parity sensitive), CelebA (smiling vs.\ gender), and HAPT-Recognition (activity vs.\ subject identity). Across all three benchmarks, GPP attains utility within roughly one percentage point of an unconstrained autoencoder baseline while reducing the adversary's AUC to near random guessing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Gaussian Privacy Protector (GPP), a framework for enhancing privacy in federated learning by mapping sensitive raw data to sanitized low-dimensional representations while preserving utility attributes.
AI.motivation: The need for effective privacy protection in federated learning settings, particularly for sensitive data from medical and wearable devices, where traditional methods may not suffice due to potential data leakage.
AI.method: GPP employs a stochastic encoder trained against a variational lower bound on mutual information pertaining to sensitive attributes, incorporating a trade-off with a cross-entropy term for utility preservation, and is adapted for federated learning by allowing local encoder training on client devices.
AI.result: GPP shows competitive utility performance, maintaining accuracy close to an unconstrained autoencoder while significantly reducing adversarial capability to infer sensitive attributes.
AI.conclusion: GPP effectively enhances privacy in federated learning while preserving data utility, marking a significant advancement for applications involving sensitive information.

title: LLM-XTM: Enhancing Cross-Lingual Topic Models with Large Language Models
summary: Cross-lingual topic modeling aims to discover shared semantic structures across languages, yet existing models depend on sparse bilingual resources and often yield incoherent or weakly aligned topics. Recent LLM-based refinements improve interpretability but are costly, document-level, and prone to hallucination, with prior white-box approaches requiring inaccessible token probabilities. We propose LLM-XTM, a framework that integrates LLM-guided topic refinement with self-consistency uncertainty quantification, enabling black-box, stable, and scalable enhancement of cross-lingual topic models. Experiments on multilingual corpora show that LLM-XTM achieves superior topic coherence and alignment while reducing reliance on bilingual dictionaries and expensive LLM calls.

AI.tldr: LLM-XTM enhances cross-lingual topic modeling by integrating LLM-guided refinement and uncertainty quantification, improving coherence and alignment while reducing resource dependency.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-lingual topic models are hindered by sparse bilingual resources and issues of coherence, alignment, and hallucination, necessitating a more effective approach.
AI.method: LLM-XTM combines LLM-guided topic refinement with self-consistency uncertainty quantification to provide a stable and scalable framework for topic modeling across languages.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that LLM-XTM achieves better topic coherence and alignment compared to previous models, while minimizing the need for bilingual resources and expensive LLM usage.
AI.conclusion: LLM-XTM presents a novel enhancement to cross-lingual topic modeling, addressing key challenges and demonstrating improved performance.

title: Mitigating the reconstruction-detection trade-off in VAE-based unsupervised anomaly detection
summary: Variational autoencoders are widely used for unsupervised anomaly detection. Model selection however remains an open-question: to remain fully unsupervised, hyperparameters are often chosen to minimize the reconstruction error on normal samples. In this paper, we reveal a trade-off between reconstruction quality and anomaly detection among $β$-VAE models. Models with constrained latent space reach higher detection metrics but lower reconstruction quality. We also assess the performance variability across random seeds and show it is linked to the distance between normal and abnormal latent distributions. From this analysis, we justify and investigate two methods to mitigate the reconstructiondetection tradeoff: beta-scheduling and the Sparse VAE. The latter especially shows an improvement in detection while maintaining high reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the trade-off between reconstruction quality and anomaly detection in variational autoencoders, proposing methods to improve detection metrics without compromising reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: To address the unresolved issue of model selection for unsupervised anomaly detection in variational autoencoders, particularly in balancing reconstruction quality and detection performance.
AI.method: The paper analyzes $β$-VAE models, examining their performance variability and its relation to latent distribution distances, while proposing two techniques (beta-scheduling and Sparse VAE) to mitigate the identified trade-off.
AI.result: It finds that constrained latent space improves detection metrics but reduces reconstruction quality, and demonstrates that Sparse VAE enhances detection while preserving reconstruction integrity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods provide strategies to balance reconstruction quality and anomaly detection capabilities, suggesting promising directions for future work.

title: Robust Beamforming for Cell-Free Systems with Parallel-Plate-Waveguided Dynamic Metasurfaces
summary: Dynamic Metasurface Antennas (DMAs) constitute a promising solution for extremely large antenna arrays, requiring lower power consumption and reduced hardware cost as compared to conventional phased arrays. In this paper, we consider a cell-free Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) system comprising multiple Base Stations (BSs) equipped with parallel-plate-waveguided DMAs, which aims to serve multiple users in the downlink direction. Focusing on a realistic frequency-selective model for the response-tunable elements of each DMA panel, and targeting to surpass the necessity of centralized designs that rely on a central processing unit with high computational power, we present a distributed optimization framework with minimal control information exchange for the frequency-selective analog and digital beamforming matrices of the multiple BSs, having the system spectral efficiency maximization as the design objective. Considering imperfect Channel State Information (CSI) availability at each BS, we devise a parallel decomposition framework for the configuration of the tunable parameters of each DMA-based BS. Our numerical results showcase the robustness of the proposed distributed beamforming design over different CSI conditions, and quantify the critical role of taking into account mutual coupling during the DMA design process.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a distributed optimization framework for Dynamic Metasurface Antennas (DMAs) in a cell-free OFDM system, aimed at maximizing spectral efficiency while accommodating for imperfect Channel State Information (CSI).
AI.motivation: The need for lower power consumption and reduced hardware costs in large antenna arrays, alongside limitations of centralized systems requiring high computational power.
AI.method: A distributed optimization framework focusing on frequency-selective analog and digital beamforming matrices for multiple Base Stations, with minimal control information exchange.
AI.result: The proposed design demonstrates robustness under varying CSI conditions and emphasizes the significance of mutual coupling in DMA design.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the effectiveness of decentralized beamforming techniques in enhancing the performance of cell-free OFDM systems with DMAs.

title: Jiao: Bridging Isolation and Customization in Mixed Criticality Robotics
comment: Accepted by Infocom'26 Embodied Intelligence Networks workshop
summary: Consumer robotics demands consolidation of safety-critical control, perception pipelines, and user applications on shared multicore platforms. While static partitioning hypervisors provide hardware-enforced isolation, directly transplanting automotive architectures encounters an expertise asymmetry problem in which end-users modifying robot behavior lack the systems knowledge that platform developers possess. We present an architecture addressing this challenge through three integrated components. A Safe IO Cell provides hardware-level override capability. A Parameter Synchronization Service encapsulates cross-domain complexity. A Safety Communication Layer implements IEC~61508-aligned verification. Our empirical evaluation on an ARM Cortex-A55 platform demonstrates that partition isolation reduces cycle-period jitter by 84.5\% and cuts tail timing error by nearly an order of magnitude (p99 $|$jitter$|$ from 69.0\,$μ$s to 7.8\,$μ$s), eliminating all $>$50\,$μ$s~excursions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an innovative architecture for consumer robotics that enhances control, safety, and usability on multicore platforms, addressing expertise asymmetry between end-users and developers.
AI.motivation: To tackle the expertise asymmetry issue in modifying robotic behavior within safety-critical applications on shared multicore platforms.
AI.method: The architecture consists of three components: a Safe IO Cell for hardware-level override, a Parameter Synchronization Service to manage complexity, and a Safety Communication Layer for IEC 61508-aligned verification.
AI.result: The empirical evaluation on ARM Cortex-A55 shows significant improvement in timing performance: 84.5% reduction in cycle-period jitter and a 90% reduction in tail timing error (p99 jitter from 69.0 μs to 7.8 μs), eliminating excursions over 50 μs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively consolidates safety and usability, ensuring reliable operation of consumer robotics in safety-critical environments.

title: AI Advocate: Educational Path to Transform Squads to the Future
summary: This paper analyzes the strategic education process aimed at transitioning traditional software development squads into hybrid structures centered on collaborative work between humans and Artificial Intelligence (AI). In a context where human-AI collaboration can significantly increase productivity, this study explores how the upskilling of XPTO professionals, referred to as AI Advocates, acts as a catalyst for cultural and technical transformation. The objective is to present an experience report on the education and enablement process of AI Advocates within a private Brazilian technology company, highlighting key lessons learned and identified challenges.

AI.tldr: This study examines the transformation of software development teams into hybrid structures that integrate human and AI collaboration, focusing on the education of AI Advocates in a Brazilian tech company.
AI.motivation: To enhance productivity through effective human-AI collaboration by upskilling professionals.
AI.method: An experience report detailing the education and enablement process of AI Advocates.
AI.result: Identified key lessons learned and challenges faced in transforming traditional teams.
AI.conclusion: The upskilling process significantly contributes to cultural and technical transformation in software development.

title: VEBench:Benchmarking Large Multimodal Models for Real-World Video Editing
summary: Real-world video editing demands not only expert knowledge of cinematic techniques but also multimodal reasoning to select, align, and combine footage into coherent narratives. While recent Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have shown remarkable progress in general video understanding, their abilities in multi-video reasoning and operational editing workflows remain largely unexplored. We introduce VEBENCH, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both editing knowledge understanding and operational reasoning in realistic video editing scenarios. VEBENCH contains 3.9K high-quality edited videos (over 257 hours) and 3,080 human-verified QA pairs, built through a three-round human-AI collaborative annotation pipeline that ensures precise temporal labeling and semantic consistency. It features two complementary QA tasks: 1) Video Editing Technique Recognition, assessing models' ability to identify 7 editing techniques using multimodal cues; and 2) Video Editing Operation Simulation, modeling real-world editing workflows by requiring the selection and temporal localization of relevant clips from multiple candidates. Extensive experiments across proprietary (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro) and open-source LMMs reveal a large gap between current model performance and human-level editing cognition. These results highlight the urgent need for bridging video understanding with creative operational reasoning. We envision VEBENCH as a foundation for advancing intelligent video editing systems and driving future research on complex reasoning.

AI.tldr: VEBENCH is a new benchmark for evaluating video editing knowledge and reasoning skills in large multimodal models, highlighting significant performance gaps compared to human editors.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of evaluation tools for video editing knowledge and operational reasoning in LMMs.
AI.method: VEBENCH includes 3.9K edited videos and 3,080 QA pairs developed via a human-AI collaborative process, featuring tasks for editing technique recognition and workflow simulation.
AI.result: Experiments indicate a significant performance gap between LMMs and human capabilities in video editing cognition.
AI.conclusion: VEBENCH aims to enhance intelligent video editing systems and encourage further research in multimodal reasoning.

title: Revisiting JBShield: Breaking and Rebuilding Representation-Level Jailbreak Defenses
summary: Defending large language models (LLMs) against jailbreak attacks, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG), remains a challenge, particularly under adaptive threat models where an attacker directly targets the defense mechanism. JBShield, a recent jailbreak defense with a 0% attack success rate in some settings, detects malicious prompts via two concept signals, a toxic concept and a jailbreak concept. We design JB-GCG, which modifies GCG's objective to combine two terms: refusal-direction suppression via cosine similarity between the refusal direction and hidden-state representations, and toxic-concept regularization via JBShield's own toxic concept score. Across five configurations on Llama-3-8B, JB-GCG achieves an average ASR of 46.2%, reaching up to 53.4% in the strongest setting. We further show that our attack remains effective against JBShield-M, achieving ASR up to 30.7% across evaluated settings. The attack persists across multiple JBShield recalibrations, confirming that the vulnerability is structural rather than calibration-specific. We analyze the cosine-similarity signatures of jailbreak representations and find that they occupy a distinctive region in refusal-direction fingerprint space that neither harmless nor harmful prompts inhabit. We introduce Representation Trajectory Verification (RTV), a new defense based on Mahalanobis outlier detection over multi-layer refusal-direction fingerprints. RTV attains an AUROC of 0.99 against our attack. Finally, we design and evaluate an additional adaptive attack against RTV with full white-box knowledge of the defense; the best attack achieves only 7% ASR at 13x the computational cost. Our results show that strong non-adaptive detection does not imply robustness under adaptive threat models, and that multi-layer representation consistency is a more reliable foundation for jailbreak detection than single-layer concept similarity.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the vulnerability of large language models to jailbreak attacks, presenting a new attack method, JB-GCG, and a defense mechanism called Representation Trajectory Verification (RTV).
AI.motivation: To address challenges in defending large language models against jailbreak attacks, particularly in adaptive threat scenarios targeting defensive mechanisms.
AI.method: The study proposes JB-GCG, which enhances the original Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack through refusal-direction suppression and toxic-concept regularization. Additionally, it introduces RTV for detection using Mahalanobis outlier detection across multi-layer representations.
AI.result: JB-GCG achieves average attack success rates (ASR) of 46.2%, peaking at 53.4%, while the attack against JBShield-M yields 30.7% ASR. RTV demonstrates high detection efficacy with an AUROC of 0.99 against JB-GCG.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that robust non-adaptive defenses are not sufficient against adaptive threats, and emphasizes the importance of multi-layer representation consistency for effective jailbreak detection.

title: SHIELD: A Diverse Clinical Note Dataset and Distilled Small Language Models for Enterprise-Scale De-identification
summary: De-identification of clinical text remains essential for secondary use of electronic health records (EHRs), yet public benchmarks such as i2b2 2006/2014 are over a decade old and lack the semantic and demographic diversity of modern narratives. While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot extraction, enterprise deployment is hindered by compute costs and governance restricting Protected Health Information (PHI) from cloud APIs. We introduce SHIELD (Synthetic Human-annotated Identifier-replaced Entries for Learning and De-identification), a diverse dataset of 1,394 notes with 10,505 gold-standard PHI spans across 9 categories, built via set-cover diversity sampling with human-in-the-loop adjudication. We evaluate four LLMs (two proprietary, two open-weight) to establish a performance ceiling, then distill these capabilities into locally deployable Small Language Models (SLMs). Distributional analysis using Frechet Text Distance and Jensen-Shannon Divergence confirms SHIELD occupies a distinct region of biomedical embedding and vocabulary space versus legacy benchmarks. Our best distilled model matches its teacher on structured PHI categories (DATE, DOCTOR, ID, PATIENT, PHONE) and achieves micro-averaged span-level precision of 0.88 and recall of 0.86 on standard workstation hardware. Cross-dataset evaluation shows diversity-trained models generalize well on universal structured PHI, while institution-specific entities remain hard to transfer, suggesting optimal deployment combines broad-coverage models with specialized models for high-volume notes. We publicly release the SHIELD dataset and the distilled DeBERTa v3 model.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SHIELD, a diverse dataset for de-identification of clinical texts, created to enhance the secondary use of EHRs, and evaluates its performance against existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of outdated public benchmarks for de-identifying clinical texts and the computational challenges posed by LLMs in practical applications.
AI.method: SHIELD comprises 1,394 clinical notes with identified PHI spans, created using set-cover diversity sampling and human adjudication, and evaluates four different LLMs to inform the development of locally deployable SLMs.
AI.result: The study finds that the best distilled model matches its more complex teacher on structured PHI categories with high precision and recall, and that diversity-trained models generalize well across datasets while specialized models are needed for specific entities.
AI.conclusion: The SHIELD dataset and distilled DeBERTa v3 model provide significant improvements in the de-identification of clinical texts, and their release supports further research in this area.

title: Graph-Spectral Fusion of Wavelet Packets and Higher-Order Statistics for Anomaly Detection in Industrial IoT Networks
summary: Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) networks demand reliable anomaly detection under harsh wireless conditions, yet most detectors fail on four fronts: hostile fading, stealthy non-Gaussian faults, discarded spatial structure, or constrained edge hardware. We propose Graph WPT+HOS, a classical label-free detector that fuses three complementary views: the Graph Fourier Transform (GFT) for spatial inconsistency, the Wavelet Packet Transform (WPT) for transient time-frequency localization, and Higher-Order Statistics (HOS) for non-Gaussian shape. The fused features are scored by a Mahalanobis distance with Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage and converted to alarms by a one-sided CUSUM. The pipeline is asymptotically optimal at the decision stage, requires no labeled anomalies, and runs on ARM-class edge hardware without GPU acceleration. Across six baselines and four domain-shift regimes under Rayleigh fading, Graph WPT+HOS attains the highest ROC-AUC and PR-AUC and reduces CUSUM detection latency.

AI.tldr: Graph WPT+HOS is a novel label-free anomaly detection method for IIoT networks, combining Graph Fourier Transform, Wavelet Packet Transform, and Higher-Order Statistics, achieving optimal performance under challenging conditions.
AI.motivation: IIoT networks need reliable anomaly detection in adverse wireless environments but current methods struggle due to various challenges.
AI.method: Graph WPT+HOS integrates Graph Fourier Transform for spatial analysis, Wavelet Packet Transform for time-frequency localization, and Higher-Order Statistics for handling non-Gaussian anomalies, using Mahalanobis distance for scoring.
AI.result: The method outperforms six existing baselines in ROC-AUC and PR-AUC metrics and minimizes detection latency across different domain shifts in Rayleigh fading environments.
AI.conclusion: Graph WPT+HOS is efficient and robust for anomaly detection in IIoT, enabling deployment on ARM-class edge devices without GPU.

title: Feasibility-aware Hybrid Control for Motion Planning under Signal Temporal Logics
summary: In this work, a novel method for planar task and motion planning based on hybrid modeling is proposed. By virtue of a discrete variable which models local constraint satisfaction and enables local feasibility analysis, the proposed control architecture unifies planning with control design. Concurrently, control barrier functions are designed on a transformed disk version of the original nonconvex and geometrically complex robotic workspace, thus amending the issue of deadlocks. Simulations of the proposed method indicate effective handling of multiple overlapping spatio-temporal tasks even in the face of input saturation.

AI.tldr: A new method for planar task and motion planning that integrates planning and control design using hybrid modeling is introduced, effectively managing overlapping tasks and preventing deadlocks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance planar task and motion planning in robotic systems, addressing local constraint satisfaction and complex workspace challenges.
AI.method: The method involves a discrete variable for local feasibility analysis and control barrier functions on a transformed workspace, improving planning and control integration.
AI.result: Simulations demonstrate the method's effectiveness in managing multiple overlapping spatio-temporal tasks, even with input saturation issues.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses planning complexity and deadlock issues, providing a robust solution for robotic task and motion planning.

title: FACTOR: Counterfactual Training-Free Test-Time Adaptation for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection often fails under distribution shifts, as it can be misled by spurious correlations between non-causal visual attributes (e.g., brightness, texture) and object categories. Existing test-time adaptation (TTA) methods either depend on costly online optimization or perform global calibration, overlooking the attribute-specific nature of these failures. To address this, we propose FACTOR (counterFACtual training-free Test-time adaptation for Open-vocabulaRy object detection), a lightweight framework grounded in counterfactual reasoning. By perturbing test images along non-causal attributes and comparing region-level predictions between original and counterfactual views, FACTOR quantifies attribute sensitivity, semantic relevance, and prediction variation to selectively suppress attribute-dependent predictions-without parameter updates. Experiments on PASCAL-C, COCO-C, and FoggyCityscapes show that FACTOR consistently outperforms prior TTA methods, demonstrating that explicit counterfactual reasoning effectively improves robustness under distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: FACTOR is a novel test-time adaptation framework for open-vocabulary object detection that enhances robustness against distribution shifts by utilizing counterfactual reasoning to address failures related to non-causal visual attributes.
AI.motivation: To tackle failures in open-vocabulary object detection caused by spurious correlations between non-causal attributes and object categories during distribution shifts.
AI.method: FACTOR utilizes a counterfactual reasoning approach, perturbing test images along non-causal attributes to assess and suppress attribute-dependent predictions without requiring parameter updates.
AI.result: FACTOR demonstrates superior performance compared to existing TTA methods on datasets like PASCAL-C, COCO-C, and FoggyCityscapes, indicating its effectiveness in improving model robustness.
AI.conclusion: The use of explicit counterfactual reasoning in FACTOR significantly enhances the resilience of object detection systems against distribution shifts.

title: PIIGuard: Mitigating PII Harvesting under Adversarial Sanitization
summary: Browsing-enabled LLM assistants can fetch webpages and answer contact-seeking queries, creating a practical channel for scraping contact-style personally identifiable information (PII) from public pages. Many prior defenses are deployed at the model, service, or agent layer rather than at the webpage itself, leaving ordinary page owners with limited deployable options. We present PIIGuard, a webpage-level defense that repurposes indirect prompt injection as a protective mechanism: the page owner embeds optimized hidden HTML fragments that steer the model away from verbatim or reconstructible disclosure of contact PII. PIIGuard searches over fragment text and insertion position using rule-based leakage scoring, evolutionary mutation, and final judge-based recoverability assessment. In direct-HTML evaluation on three target models (GPT-5.4-nano, Claude-haiku-4.5, and DeepSeek-chat(latest v3.2)), PIIGuard achieves at least 97.0% defense success rate under both rule-based and judge-based leakage evaluation, often reaching 100.0%, while preserving benign same-page QA utility. We further evaluate two harder settings: public-URL browsing and attacker-side LLM sanitization of fetched webpage. These results show that page-side defensive fragments can remain effective in deployment for some model-position pairs, but robustness varies substantially across browsing interfaces and sanitizer prompts. Overall, PIIGuard demonstrates that page owners can use page-side fragments as a practical mitigation for web-grounded PII leakage.

AI.tldr: PIIGuard is a webpage-level defense system that uses hidden HTML fragments to prevent large language models (LLMs) from disclosing personally identifiable information (PII) while preserving webpage utility.
AI.motivation: To provide webpage owners with deployable options to protect against PII leaks, focusing on defenses that operate directly at the webpage level rather than at the model or service level.
AI.method: PIIGuard employs optimized hidden HTML fragments that redirect model queries, using techniques like rule-based leakage scoring and evolutionary mutation to evaluate fragment effectiveness and assess recoverability.
AI.result: PIIGuard shows a defense success rate of at least 97% against three LLMs in direct-HTML evaluations, achieving up to 100% in some cases, and retains functionality for benign queries on the same page.
AI.conclusion: The study illustrates that webpage owners can effectively mitigate PII leakage by embedding defensive measures at the page level, though effectiveness can vary based on model and browsing context.

title: When to Think, When to Speak: Learning Disclosure Policies for LLM Reasoning
summary: In single-stream autoregressive interfaces, the same tokens both update the model state and constitute an irreversible public commitment. This coupling creates a \emph{silence tax}: additional deliberation postpones the first \emph{task-relevant} content, while naive early streaming risks premature commitments that bias subsequent generations. We introduce \textbf{\emph{Side-by-Side (SxS)}} Interleaved Reasoning, which makes \emph{disclosure timing} a controllable decision within standard autoregressive generation. SxS interleaves partial disclosures with continued private reasoning in the same context, but releases content only when it is \emph{supported} by the reasoning so far. To learn such pacing without incentivizing filler, we construct entailment-aligned interleaved trajectories by matching answer prefixes to supporting reasoning prefixes, then train with SFT to acquire the dual-action semantics and RL to recover reasoning performance under the new format. Across two Qwen3 architectures/scales (MoE \textbf{Qwen3-30B-A3B}, dense \textbf{Qwen3-4B}) and both in-domain (AIME25) and out-of-domain (GPQA-Diamond) benchmarks, SxS improves accuracy--\emph{content-latency} Pareto trade-offs under token-level proxies (e.g., inter-update waiting).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Side-by-Side (SxS) Interleaved Reasoning, a method that manages the timing of disclosure in autoregressive models to balance accuracy and content latency in response generation.
AI.motivation: The challenge of irreversible commitments in single-stream autoregressive interfaces leads to a silence tax, where the timing of task-relevant content is crucial for performance.
AI.method: SxS interleaves partial disclosures with private reasoning, releasing content only when it is supported by previous reasoning. It uses entailment-aligned trajectories for training, combining supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.
AI.result: SxS demonstrates improvements in accuracy and latency trade-offs on various benchmarks and models, including the MoE Qwen3-30B-A3B and dense Qwen3-4B across in-domain and out-of-domain datasets.
AI.conclusion: The SxS method successfully enhances the reasoning capabilities of autoregressive models by allowing for better timing control of content disclosure.

title: DeRelayL: Sustainable Decentralized Relay Learning
comment: 24 pages, 4 figures, Published in IEEE Transaction on Mobile Computing
summary: In the era of big data, large-scale machine learning models have revolutionized various fields, driving significant advancements. However, large-scale model training demands high financial and computational resources, which are only affordable by a few technological giants and well-funded institutions. In this case, common users like mobile users, the real creators of valuable data, are often excluded from fully benefiting due to the barriers, while the current methods for accessing large-scale models either limit user ownership or lack sustainability. This growing gap highlights the urgent need for a collaborative model training approach, allowing common users to train and share models. However, existing collaborative model training paradigms, especially federated learning (FL), primarily focus on data privacy and group-based model aggregation. To this end, this paper intends to address this issue by proposing a novel training paradigm named decentralized relay learning (DeRelayL), a sustainable learning system where permissionless participants can contribute to model training in a relay-like manner and share the model. In detail, this paper presents the architecture and workflow of DeRelayL, designs incentive mechanisms to ensure sustainability, and conducts theoretical analysis and numerical simulations to demonstrate its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces decentralized relay learning (DeRelayL), a novel collaborative model training paradigm aimed at inclusivity for common users in the era of big data, facilitating sustainable model training and sharing.
AI.motivation: Address the exclusion of common users from large-scale model training due to high resource demands and current limitations in collaborative paradigms.
AI.method: Proposes decentralized relay learning (DeRelayL), which allows permissionless participants to train models in a relay manner and share the results, ensuring participant incentives for sustainability.
AI.result: Demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of DeRelayL through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations.
AI.conclusion: DeRelayL offers a sustainable and inclusive approach to collaborative model training, benefiting common users and enhancing the accessibility of large-scale machine learning.

title: Joint Scheduling of Sensing Data Offloading and Edge Inference for Multi-UAV Networks
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) often collaborate by collecting and offloading sensing streams to an edge server, where a deep neural network (DNN) model performs cross-stream alignment, fusion, and inference. However, the coupling between wireless offloading and DNN execution makes end-to-end latency minimization challenging. To address this issue, this paper investigates efficient edge inference in multi-UAV networks. Specifically, a multi-UAV collaborative edge inference model is first established, in which UAV sensing streams are processed by a multi-branch DNN on a multi-core accelerator. Based on this model, an end-to-end latency minimization problem with a synchronization penalty is formulated. A genetic algorithm (GA)-based full joint scheduler, termed \texttt{GA-Joint}, is then developed to obtain high-quality scheduling solutions. To reduce the search complexity, two lightweight variants, termed \texttt{GA-DAG} and \texttt{GA-DACS}, are further proposed. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed GA-based scheduling algorithms achieve lower end-to-end latency than \texttt{Decoupled-Greedy} and \texttt{Joint-Greedy}, which represent decoupled and joint greedy scheduling schemes, respectively, in most cases. Furthermore, \texttt{GA-DACS} achieves performance close to that of \texttt{GA-Joint} in many cases and even delivers slightly lower latency in certain scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses end-to-end latency minimization in multi-UAV networks that collaborate for edge inference using DNNs, proposing a genetic algorithm-based scheduling approach.
AI.motivation: The coupling between wireless offloading and DNN execution in UAV networks complicates latency minimization.
AI.method: A multi-UAV collaborative edge inference model with a multi-branch DNN is established, and a GA-based full joint scheduler, 	exttt{GA-Joint}, is developed alongside two lightweight variants, 	exttt{GA-DAG} and 	exttt{GA-DACS}.
AI.result: The proposed GA-based scheduling algorithms outperform 	exttt{Decoupled-Greedy} and 	exttt{Joint-Greedy}, particularly in achieving lower end-to-end latency.
AI.conclusion: exttt{GA-DACS} performs comparably to 	exttt{GA-Joint} and even slightly better in some scenarios, indicating an effective solution for minimizing latency in UAV collaborative inference.

title: Sensorless State Estimation and Control for Agile Cable-Suspended Payload Transport by Quadrotors
summary: This work proposes a novel control and estimation approach for aerial manipulation of a cable-suspended load using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). Common approaches in the state of the art have practical limitations, relying on direct load measurements and Lagrangian methods for dynamic modeling. The lack of a straightforward dynamic model of the system led us to propose adopting the Udwadia-Kalaba method to explicitly incorporate the cable's geometric constraints. This formulation allowed for the consistent derivation of the tension force and its direct integration into the NMPC prediction model. Additionally, we propose a sensorless load state estimation based on the same geometric constraints. Results from real-robot experiments demonstrated that the explicit inclusion of load dynamics in the optimization problem significantly reduces trajectory-tracking errors and yields better overall performance compared to strategies based on incomplete models.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new method for controlling UAVs manipulating cable-suspended loads by incorporating geometric constraints into the dynamic modeling, improving performance and trajectory tracking.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the limitations of existing methods that rely on direct load measurements and Lagrangian methods, which do not provide a straightforward dynamic model for cable-suspended loads.
AI.method: The Udwadia-Kalaba method is employed to explicitly include the cable's geometric constraints, allowing for the derivation of the tension force and its integration into a Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) model. A sensorless load state estimation technique is also proposed utilizing these geometric constraints.
AI.result: Experiments with real robots showed that the integration of load dynamics into the optimization process significantly reduced trajectory-tracking errors compared to traditional methods based on incomplete models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates improved control and estimation of UAVs in aerial manipulation tasks, validating the effectiveness of incorporating geometric constraints into dynamic modeling.

title: Beyond Rules: LLM-Powered Linting for Quantum Programs
summary: As quantum computing transitions from theoretical experimentation to its practical application, the reliability of quantum software has become a critical bottleneck. Traditional static analysis techniques for quantum programs, primarily rule-based linters, are increasingly inadequate; they struggle to keep pace with rapidly evolving APIs and fail to capture complex, context-dependent quantum programming problems. This results in high maintenance overhead and limited detection capabilities. In this paper, we introduce LintQ-LLM+CoT and LintQ-LLM+RAG, novel approaches that redefine the detection of quantum programming problems by employing Large Language Models (LLMs) specialized, respectively, via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system that grounds the model's reasoning in a curated knowledge base of verified quantum programming problems and best practices. We conducted a rigorous and manual comparative evaluation against the state-of-the-art rule-based tool, LintQ, using a corpus of 55 Qiskit programs. Our results show that LLM-based approaches, with and without RAG, outperform LintQ in terms of quantum programming problems detection correctness (precision) and completeness (recall). Overall, LLM-based approaches were more effective than LintQ (F1-score equal to 0.70 and 0.68 vs. 0.41). Furthermore, the RAG-enhanced variant demonstrated a slightly superior precision, effectively reducing false positives. Our findings suggest that LLMs provide a scalable and adaptive foundation for the next generation of linters in quantum software engineering.

AI.tldr: This paper presents two novel LLM-based approaches for detecting quantum programming issues, outperforming traditional rule-based methods.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional static analysis tools in efficiently detecting quantum programming problems due to rapid API changes and complexity.
AI.method: Introduction of LintQ-LLM+CoT and LintQ-LLM+RAG, leveraging Large Language Models with Chain-of-Thought prompting and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation system for grounded reasoning.
AI.result: The LLM-based approaches surpassed the rule-based tool LintQ in precision and recall, achieving F1-scores of 0.70 and 0.68 compared to LintQ's 0.41, with RAG variant reducing false positives.
AI.conclusion: LLMs represent a promising solution for enhancing the detection capabilities in quantum software engineering, providing scalability and adaptability.

title: KVerus: Scalable and Resilient Formal Verification Proof Generation for Rust Code
summary:
Formal verification provides the highest assurance of software correctness and security, but its application to large-scale, evolving systems remains a major challenge. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating proof generation, they often fail in real-world settings due to their inability to handle complex cross-module dependencies or changes in the codebase or the verification toolchain. We identify the fundamental problem as the Semantic-Structural Gap: LLMs operate on semantic code patterns, whereas formal verification is governed by rigid structural dependencies, a disconnect that leads to brittle, unsustainable proofs.
  To bridge this gap, we propose a new paradigm of self-adaptive verification and present KVerus, a retrieval-augmented system for Verus-based Rust verification that can adapt to an evolving software environment. KVerus constructs a dynamic knowledge base of code metadata, lemma semantics, and toolchain specifics. By combining dependency-aware program analysis, semantic lemma indexing, and error-driven self-refinement, it can navigate intricate cross-file dependencies to synthesize proofs and automatically repair proofs when faced with common evolutionary changes. Across three single-file benchmarks, KVerus verifies 80.2% of tasks, outperforming the state-of-the-art AutoVerus (56.9%) and degrades less than AutoVerus under breaking Verus updates. On three repository-level benchmarks with cross-file dependencies, KVerus achieves a 51.0% success rate, compared to 4.5% for a multi-round prompting baseline. Finally, on the Asterinas Rust OS kernel, KVerus produces upstream-accepted proofs that verify 23 previously unverified functions (21.0% of proof code) in the memory-management module. KVerus represents a significant step towards making formal verification a scalable and sustainable practice for modern, security-critical software.

AI.tldr: KVerus is a self-adaptive verification system for Rust that overcomes limitations of large language models by efficiently handling complex dependencies in evolving software for formal verification.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges faced by large-scale, evolving systems in formal verification due to the Semantic-Structural Gap between LLMs and formal verification requirements.
AI.method: KVerus employs a dynamic knowledge base integrating code metadata, lemma semantics, and toolchain specifics, utilizing dependency-aware analysis, semantic indexing, and error-driven refinement for proof synthesis and repair.
AI.result: KVerus verified 80.2% of tasks in single-file benchmarks compared to 56.9% for AutoVerus, and 51.0% success on repository-level benchmarks with complex dependencies, superior to 4.5% of baseline.
AI.conclusion: KVerus advances formal verification as a scalable practice for security-critical software by producing accepted proofs for previously unverified functions.

title: TACO: Trajectory Aligning Cross-view Optimisation
summary: Cross-View Geo-localisation (CVGL) matches ground imagery against satellite tiles to give absolute position fixes, an alternative to GNSS where signals are occluded, jammed, or spoofed. Recent fine-grained CVGL methods regress sub-tile metric pose, but have only been evaluated as one-shot localisers, never as the primary fix in a live pipeline. Inertial sensing provides high-rate relative motion, but accumulates unbounded drift without an absolute anchor. We propose TACO, a tightly-coupled IMU + fine-grained CVGL pipeline that consumes a single GNSS reading at start-up and thereafter operates on onboard sensing alone. A closed-form cross-track error model triggers CVGL before IMU drift exceeds the matcher's capture radius, and a forward-biased five-point multi-crop search keeps inference cost fixed at five forward passes per fix. A yaw-residual gate rejects fixes that disagree with the onboard compass, and an anisotropic body-frame noise model scales each Unscented Kalman Filter update by per-fix confidence. A factor graph with vetted loop closures provides an offline smoothed trajectory. On the KITTI raw dataset, TACO reduces median Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) from 97.0m (IMU-only) to 16.3m, a 5.9 times reduction, at <0.1 ms per-frame fusion cost and a 5-10% camera duty cycle. Code is available: github.com/tavisshore/TACO.

AI.tldr: TACO enhances geolocation through a tightly-coupled IMU and fine-grained CVGL, significantly improving trajectory accuracy while maintaining low computational cost.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of GNSS in scenarios with signal interference and to improve the trajectory accuracy of ground imagery localization against satellite tiles without accumulating significant drift from inertial sensors.
AI.method: TACO integrates IMU data with a CVGL pipeline, starting with a GNSS reading and employing a closed-form cross-track error model to manage drift and maintain accuracy, using a multi-crop search and a yaw-residual gate for fix verification and noise scaling in updates.
AI.result: TACO reduces median Absolute Trajectory Error from 97.0m without GNSS to 16.3m with the proposed method, demonstrating a substantial 5.9 times improvement in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a reliable and efficient alternative for geo-localization in environments where GNSS signals are compromised, achieving significant accuracy increases at a low computational cost.

title: Zero Day Attacks: Novel Behaviour or Novel Vulnerability?
summary: Zero-day attacks pose severe cybersecurity risks due to their high success rates and stealth. Because signature-based approaches struggle to detect such attacks, building Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) for detecting zero-day attacks is essential. We contend that for an IDS to be effective it must be grounded in an understanding of how zero-day attacks manifest in real-world networks. To this end, we review documented zero-day incidents spanning 20 years, finding that these attacks arise from the exploitation of undisclosed vulnerabilities rather than novel attack behavior. Guided by this insight, we propose a taxonomy of zero-day vulnerability types and analyze assumptions of ML-based intrusion detection approaches. Our analysis shows that incidents consistently involve vulnerability exploitation, with memory-corruption flaws being most used; additionally, attacks targeting defensive-mechanism vulnerabilities have increased in recent years. We also identify a mismatch: while incident reports emphasize vulnerability exploitation, many ML-based detectors are designed to detect hypothetical "novel behaviors" during attack execution. Our findings indicate that vulnerability-centric methods are more aligned with real-world attack mechanisms. Consequently, reliance on behavior-based detection alone may overstate zero-day detection capabilities in ML-based IDSs. We advocate for cautious interpretation of such claims and call for improved automated vulnerability detection frameworks aligned with real-world exploit characteristics. Effective defense against zero-day attacks requires prioritizing vulnerability-centeric approaches that enable early identification and mitigation across the lifecycle. The ability to detect attacks that utilize novel behaviors (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)) is useful, but it does necessarily equate to the ability to detect zero-day attacks.

AI.tldr: This paper emphasizes the importance of vulnerability-centric approaches in Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) to effectively detect zero-day attacks, which primarily exploit unknown vulnerabilities rather than exhibiting novel behaviors.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to enhance the effectiveness of IDSs against zero-day attacks by understanding their real-world manifestation and the exploitation of undisclosed vulnerabilities.
AI.method: A review of documented zero-day incidents over 20 years is conducted, leading to the development of a taxonomy of zero-day vulnerability types and an analysis of machine learning (ML) based detection assumptions.
AI.result: Findings reveal that attacks predominantly exploit vulnerabilities, particularly memory-corruption flaws, and that the focus on detecting novel behaviors in ML-based systems misaligns with real-world exploitation patterns.
AI.conclusion: The study advocates for a shift towards vulnerability-centric detection frameworks to improve the identification and mitigation of zero-day threats, cautioning against over-reliance on behavior-based detection.

title: From prompting to evidence-based translation: A RAG+prompt system for Japanese-Chinese translation and its pedagogical potential
summary: Large language models perform well on high-resource pairs but are less reliable for Japanese-Chinese sentences containing noun-modifying clause constructions (NMCCs). This study evaluates a retrieval-augmented generation RAG+Prompt translation system that integrates linguistic analysis, embedding-based retrieval, prompt construction, and LLM generation without modifying the base model. The analysis module outputs A1 (inner vs. outer NMCC) and A2 (risk predictions: lexical choice/NMCC handling/word order/style/register); top-k = 5 similar Ja-Zh examples (L2 distance) and A1/A2 are inserted into an enhanced prompt. Using GPT-4o and a 66-sentence test set, we compare six knowledge-base sizes (0/100/200/500/1,000/2,000). Macro-averaged sentence-level BLEU (1-4-gram with brevity penalty; cased; Chinese at the character level) is the sole metric. Mean BLEU increases from 24.28 at 0 (RAG disabled) to 29.96 at 2,000 (+5.68; +23.4%). The upward trend holds across sizes, with larger knowledge bases yielding higher scores. We conclude that the RAG+Prompt translation system improves Ja-Zh translation of sentences containing NMCCs in an interpretable and auditable manner. Limitations include one base model, one metric, and reliance on published texts and commercial APIs; future work will broaden genres, language pairs, and evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: This study presents a retrieval-augmented generation translation system that enhances Japanese-Chinese sentence translation, particularly for noun-modifying clause constructions.
AI.motivation: To address the reliability issues of large language models in translating Japanese-Chinese sentences, specifically those with complex structures like NMCCs.
AI.method: A RAG+Prompt translation system integrating linguistic analysis, retrieval, prompt construction, and LLM generation was utilized without altering the base model, evaluated with varying sizes of knowledge bases.
AI.result: The mean BLEU score improved significantly from 24.28 to 29.96 with the largest knowledge base, indicating that a larger knowledge base positively impacts translation quality.
AI.conclusion: The RAG+Prompt translation system enhances the translation of NMCC-containing sentences in a transparent manner; however, further research is required to expand genres and metrics.

title: Proteo-R1: Reasoning Foundation Models for De Novo Protein Design
summary: Deep learning in \emph{de novo} protein design has achieved atomic-level fidelity. However, existing models remain largely non-deliberative: they directly synthesize molecular geometries without explicitly reasoning about which residues or interactions are functionally essential. As a result, design decisions are entangled with continuous sampling dynamics, limiting interpretability, controllability, and systematic reuse of biochemical knowledge. We introduce \textbf{Proteo-R1}, a reasoning-guided protein design framework that explicitly decouples \emph{molecular understanding} from \emph{geometric generation}. Proteo-R1 adopts a dual-expert architecture in which a multimodal large language model (MLLM) serves as an \emph{understanding expert}, analyzing protein sequences, structures, and textual context to identify key functional residues that govern binding and specificity. These residue-level decisions are then passed as hard constraints to a separate diffusion-based \emph{generation expert}, which performs conditional co-design while respecting the fixed interaction anchors. This factorization mirrors how human experts approach molecular engineering: first, reasoning about critical interactions, then optimizing geometry subject to those constraints. By operationalizing reasoning as explicit residue-level commitments rather than latent textual guidance, Proteo-R1 achieves stable, interpretable, and modular integration of LLM reasoning with state-of-the-art geometric generative models. Code, data, and demos are available at https://smiles724.github.io/r1/.

AI.tldr: Introducing Proteo-R1, a framework for protein design that combines reasoning and geometric generation for enhanced interpretability and control.
AI.motivation: There is a need for more interpretative and controllable models in protein design that separate functional reasoning from geometric synthesis.
AI.method: Proteo-R1 uses a dual-expert model with a multimodal large language model for understanding functional residues and a diffusion-based model for geometric generation under fixed constraints.
AI.result: This approach allows for stable and interpretable co-design, aligning with expert human methodology in molecular engineering.
AI.conclusion: Proteo-R1 successfully integrates reasoning with geometric models in protein design, promoting modularity and interpretability.

title: Deterministic Sparse FFT via Keyed Multi-View Gating with $O(\sqrt{N} \log k)$ Expected Time
comment: 19 pages, 6 figures. Includes theoretical analysis, algorithm specification, and complexity proofs. Companion works establish deterministic lower bounds and hybrid safety-certified extensions
summary: We introduce a deterministic sparse Fourier transform framework based on a keyed multi-view gating mechanism that leverages 2-of-3 Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) agreement to reduce candidate frequency pairs from $O(k^2)$ to $Θ(k)$ under sparse-regime assumptions. Unlike prior approaches that rely on randomized bucketization for candidate formation, the proposed method provides deterministic structure with probabilistic guarantees arising only from assumptions on frequency placement and independence of affine hashing across views. The algorithm is realized through a peeling-based recovery procedure that extracts frequencies directly from singleton bins without explicit pair enumeration. A recursive self-reduction eliminates the $O(\sqrt{N} \log N)$ preprocessing floor, yielding $O(\sqrt{N} \log k)$ expected identification time while maintaining an $O(N \log N)$ worst-case bound via deterministic dense-FFT fallback. A multi-view verification framework combining Parseval energy consistency and bin-wise residual checks ensures bounded failure probability and no false negatives under correct verification. This establishes a framework combining deterministic candidate reduction, sublinear expected complexity, and worst-case safety guarantees within a CRT-based sparse FFT architecture.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a deterministic sparse Fourier transform framework that uses a multi-view gating mechanism and CRT for efficient frequency pair reduction, achieving sublinear identification time while ensuring worst-case guarantees.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency of frequency identification in sparse Fourier transforms by reducing the candidate selection process from quadratic to linear time without compromising safety.
AI.method: A deterministic framework utilizing a keyed multi-view gating mechanism and 2-of-3 CRT agreement, combined with a peeling-based recovery process and recursive self-reduction to optimize frequency identification.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves $O(	heta(k))$ candidate reduction and $O(	heta( rac{	ext{N}}{	ext{log k}}))$ expected identification time, while ensuring $O(N 	ext{log N})$ worst-case performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully integrates deterministic frequency candidate reduction with sublinear expected complexity and robust worst-case performance in the context of sparse Fourier transforms.

title: FUS3DMaps: Scalable and Accurate Open-Vocabulary Semantic Mapping by 3D Fusion of Voxel- and Instance-Level Layers
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Open-vocabulary semantic mapping enables robots to spatially ground previously unseen concepts without requiring predefined class sets. Current training-free methods commonly rely on multi-view fusion of semantic embeddings into a 3D map, either at the instance-level via segmenting views and encoding image crops of segments, or by projecting image patch embeddings directly into a dense semantic map. The latter approach sidesteps segmentation and 2D-to-3D instance association by operating on full uncropped image frames, but existing methods remain limited in scalability. We present FUS3DMaps, an online dual-layer semantic mapping method that jointly maintains both dense and instance-level open-vocabulary layers within a shared voxel map. This design enables further voxel-level semantic fusion of the layer embeddings, combining the complementary strengths of both semantic mapping approaches. We find that our proposed semantic cross-layer fusion approach improves the quality of both the instance-level and dense layers, while also enabling a scalable and highly accurate instance-level map where the dense layer and cross-layer fusion are restricted to a spatial sliding window. Experiments on established 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks as well as a selection of large-scale scenes show that FUS3DMaps achieves accurate open-vocabulary semantic mapping at multi-story building scales. Additional material and code will be made available: https://githanonymous.github.io/FUS3DMaps/.

AI.tldr: FUS3DMaps introduces an online dual-layer semantic mapping technique that enhances open-vocabulary semantic mapping for robots by integrating dense and instance-level layers in a shared voxel map, improving scalability and accuracy.
AI.motivation: To enhance robots' capabilities in spatially grounding unseen concepts in a scalable manner without requiring predefined class sets, addressing limitations in current training-free semantic mapping methods.
AI.method: The proposed FUS3DMaps method uses an online approach with dual-layer semantic mapping, maintaining both dense and instance-level embeddings within a shared voxel map and applying voxel-level semantic fusion.
AI.result: FUS3DMaps improves the quality of both layers and achieves accurate open-vocabulary semantic mapping, even for complex multi-story building environments, outperforming existing methods in scalability and precision.
